title: The openssl extension is required for SSL/TLS protection but is not available.
author: widmo34
body:
Hi.

I can't install any of composers modules. Not even hit command composer self-update. Previous time I set off https scanning  in avast and it worked. This this it didn't help me.  All the time kicks me with this message ->>>The openssl extension is required for SSL/TLS protection but is not available. If you can not enable the openssl extension, you can disable this error, at your own risk, by setting  the 'disable-tls' option to true. I have newest version of avast. Can someone assists ?

title: Is `content-hash` in `composer.lock` needed?
author: ro0NL
body: Without it, it would make rebasing/merging generally a lot easier.

title: Race condition in cache with multiple parallel builds
author: anomiex
body:
In our CI, we run tests and builds for multiple projects in our monorepo in parallel. Occasionally we run into an error like the following:
```
  - Downloading brain/monkey (2.7.0)
    Failed to download brain/monkey from dist: /home/runner/.cache/composer/files/brain/monkey does not exist and could not be created: 
    Source fallback is disabled. Not trying alternative sources.
::error ::/home/runner/.cache/composer/files/brain/monkey does not exist and could not be created: 

In Filesystem.php line 261:
                                                                               
  /home/runner/.cache/composer/files/brain/monkey does not exist and could no  
  t be created:                                                                
                                                                               

update [--with WITH] [--prefer-source] [--prefer-dist] [--prefer-install PREFER-INSTALL] [--dry-run] [--dev] [--no-dev] [--lock] [--no-install] [--no-audit] [--audit-format AUDIT-FORMAT] [--no-security-blocking] [--no-blocking] [--no-autoloader] [--no-suggest] [--no-progress] [-w|--with-dependencies] [-W|--with-all-dependencies] [-v|vv|vvv|--verbose] [-o|--optimize-autoloader] [-a|--classmap-authoritative] [--strict-psr-autoloader] [--apcu-autoloader] [--apcu-autoloader-prefix APCU-AUTOLOADER-PREFIX] [--ignore-platform-req IGNORE-PLATFORM-REQ] [--ignore-platform-reqs] [--prefer-stable] [--prefer-lowest] [-m|--minimal-changes] [--patch-only] [-i|--interactive] [--root-reqs] [--bump-after-update [BUMP-AFTER-UPDATE]] [--] [<packages>...]
```

Looking at the code, that seems like it's coming from
https://github.com/composer/composer/blob/8fbad13554162188f900ff149454932500d508a8/src/Composer/Util/Filesystem.php#L247-L261
I suspect the multiple `composer` processes are running into a race condition, where one process creates the directory in the cache somewhere in between the `!is_dir()` check on line 249 and the later `mkdir()` on line 260, causing the `mkdir` to fail.


My `composer.json`:

```json
n/a
```

Output of `composer diagnose`:

```
Checking pubkeys: FAIL
Missing pubkey for tags verification
Missing pubkey for dev verification
Run composer self-update --update-keys to set them up
Checking Composer version: You are not running the latest stable version, run `composer self-update` to update (2.10.0 => 2.10.2)
Composer version: 2.10.0
Checking Composer and its dependencies for vulnerabilities: OK
PHP version: 8.4.23
PHP binary path: /usr/bin/php8.4
OpenSSL version: OpenSSL 3.0.13 30 Jan 2024
curl version: 8.5.0 libz 1.3 brotli 1.1.0 zstd supported ssl OpenSSL/3.0.13 HTTP 1.0, 1.1, 2
zip: extension present, unzip present, 7-Zip present (7z)
Active plugins: automattic/composer-304-plugin
Checking composer.json: OK
Checking composer.lock: OK
Checking platform settings: OK
Checking git settings: OK git version 2.54.0
Checking http connectivity to packagist: OK
Checking https connectivity to packagist: OK
Checking github.com oauth access: OK does not expire
Checking disk free space: OK
```

When I run this command: <!-- run it with `-vvv` added to it ideally to get full debug output -->

```
composer update
```

I get the following output: <!-- FULL OUTPUT please, not just what you think is relevant -->

```
Loading composer repositories with package information
Updating dependencies
```

title: expand docs on limitations of using explicit references
author: joachim-n
body:
The docs at https://getcomposer.org/doc/04-schema.md#package-links say that using a specific commit hash has limitations.

It would be useful if this had more specific information.

For example, you can't even do `composer update --lock` reliably when using a commit hash, as it takes the requirements of that dependency from the HEAD of the branch (AFAICT!) even though, naively, you'd think that `composer update --lock` just does 'keep everything the same'.

Some of the text from this comment could be moved into the docs too: https://github.com/composer/composer/issues/6366#issuecomment-295761425

title: 'why-not' with a version number skips to newer versions
author: joachim-n
body:
My `composer.json`:

```json
is big and complicated
```

I did `composer why-not guzzlehttp/guzzle ^7.11`. I got:

```
drupal/core-recommended        11.3.10  requires         guzzlehttp/guzzle (~7.10.0)
guzzlehttp/guzzle              7.13.1   requires         guzzlehttp/promises (^2.5)
drupal-composer/drupal-project dev-main does not require guzzlehttp/promises (but 2.3.1 is installed)
guzzlehttp/guzzle              7.13.1   requires         guzzlehttp/psr7 (^2.12.3)
drupal-composer/drupal-project dev-main does not require guzzlehttp/psr7 (but 2.8.1 is installed)
Not finding what you were looking for? Try calling `composer update "guzzlehttp/guzzle:^7.11" --dry-run` to get another view on the problem.
```

Why does it tell me about guzzlehttp/guzzle 7.13.1? This is not relevant to what I am asking. If it's the case that the guzzlehttp/promises requirement is the same in 7.11 and 7.13, it should still show "7.11" in the output, because that's what's being asked about.

title: Automatically retry failed Packagist requests: covering HTTP/2 400
author: jpmschuler
body:
Can Composer automatically retry refused connections a few times before exiting? (Similar to #10161 - but adding HTTP/2 400)

```
Package operations: 132 installs, 0 updates, 0 removals
  - Downloading symfony/event-dispatcher (v8.1.1)
  - Downloading symfony/var-exporter (v8.1.1)
  - Downloading symfony/cache (v8.1.1)
    Failed to download symfony/cache from dist: The 
    "https://codeload.github.com/symfony/cache/legacy.zip/c14decc1b0755b1e8ab6babeef56e1880348e817" 
    file could not be downloaded (HTTP/2 400 )
    Source fallback is disabled. Not trying alternative sources.
In CurlDownloader.php line 674:
                                                                               
  The "https://codeload.github.com/symfony/cache/legacy.zip/c14decc1b0755b1e8  
  ab6babeef56e1880348e817" file could not be downloaded (HTTP/2 400 )          
```

GitHub seems to have a lot of hiccups recently where HTTP/2 400 are spawned (reuse of a prior working HTTP/2 connection failed) - as there are factors which could worsen the problem (e.g. a firewall with deep package inspection), still this is worth a retry like the other errors, especially as the necessary code is available.

Else bigger composer package downloads might fail, just because one package wasn't retried (Example from TYPO3 nightly jobs: https://git.typo3.org/typo3/CI/cms/-/jobs/5707898#L89).

title: Packagist dependency doesn't seem to update
author: brendt
body:
- A couple of minutes ago, I tagged a new release for a package of mine: https://github.com/tempestphp/markdown/releases/tag/1.1.1
- On Packagist I saw that the tag wasn't pulled in automatically after a minute or two, so I pressed the "update" button. After which 1.1.1 showed up: https://packagist.org/packages/tempest/markdown#1.1.1
- However, running composer with this new requirement `"tempest/markdown": "^1.1.1"` continues to fail: `Root composer.json requires tempest/markdown ^1.1.1, found tempest/markdown[dev-commonmark-spec, dev-main, dev-perf-refactor, 0.0.1, 0.0.2, 0.0.3, 1.0.0, 1.0.1, 1.1.0] but it does not match the constraint.`
- When I switch to `dev-main`, it pulls in the latest commit from before the tag from 4 days ago: https://github.com/tempestphp/markdown/commit/a89702e4873bc07cc24256aa62a389387a82ad80

Maybe this is simply a timing problem now that are more checks in place — that's ok. However, the tag is listed on packagist, which is what's confusing me and which is why I decided to make this issue.

title: `show --tree --format=json`: include installed version for sub-dependencies
author: andrew
body:
In [git-pkgs/resolve](https://github.com/git-pkgs/resolve) we build dependency graphs by shelling out to each ecosystem's package manager and parsing the output. For Composer we'd like to use `composer show --tree --format=json`, but below the top level the `version` field is the constraint from the parent's `require` block rather than the installed version:

```json
{
  "name": "guzzlehttp/guzzle",
  "version": "7.12.3",
  "requires": [
    { "name": "guzzlehttp/promises", "version": "^2.5", "requires": [ ... ] }
  ]
}
```

`guzzlehttp/promises` is installed at `2.5.0` but the tree reports `^2.5`. `composer show --format=json` without `--tree` does give installed versions for every package, but it's flat, so we currently parse `composer.lock` directly to get both the graph and resolved versions in one pass.

Would you accept a PR adding an `installed` field to each tree node alongside the existing constraint? That keeps the current field's meaning intact and lets tooling get the full picture from one command.

```sh
composer init -n --name demo/demo
composer require guzzlehttp/guzzle
composer show --tree --format=json | jq '.installed[0].requires[] | select(.name=="guzzlehttp/promises").version'
# "^2.5"
composer show --format=json | jq '.installed[] | select(.name=="guzzlehttp/promises").version'
# "2.5.0"
```

Composer 2.10.1, PHP 8.4.

title: composer audit - list of ignored CVEs from file/parameter
author: bartiii

title: A `composer update` that only updates metadata for a package, generates an upgrade to the same version without additional context
author: glaubinix
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

Having a composer.json like the below
```
{
    "repositories": [
        {
            "type": "package",
            "package": {
                "name": "acme/package",
                "version": "1.0",
                "type": "metapackage"
            }
        }
    ],
    "require": {
        "acme/package": "*"
    }
}
```

Follow the steps below
* run `composer update`
* edit the composer.json and mark the package as `"abandoned": true`
* rerun `composer update`

You will see an output similar to
```
Loading composer repositories with package information
Updating dependencies
Lock file operations: 0 installs, 1 update, 0 removals
  - Upgrading acme/package (1.0 => 1.0)
Writing lock file
Installing dependencies from lock file (including require-dev)
Package operations: 0 installs, 1 update, 0 removals
  - Upgrading acme/package (1.0 => 1.0)
Generating autoload files
No security vulnerability advisories found.
```

**Describe the solution you'd like**
While the output is correct that yes, the package metadata gets updated in the lock file (abandoned: true is added) and every lock file change leads to the package being updated. It's not 100% obvious what or why this is happening. Especially when running the update command with `--dry-run` or for someone who isn't that familiar with Composer.

I am wondering if we can either:
* display what exactly changed e.g. "package abandoned", "source URL changed"
* display a generic message e.g. "metadata changes only"
* display a generic message but have a way to show the full diff it generates for the package

title: Advisory ignore list expiry
author: wouterj

title: Composer 2.9.5 advisory polices and dev versions
author: joegl
body:
I am new to the addition of the advisory policies in Composer 2.9+ and am experiencing something a little funky. We have the `minimum-stability` set to `dev` in a project, and when I run a `composer update` it is upgrading packages with active CVE's revealed in `composer audit` to dev versions that are on the same commit as their latest release. For reference, this is a Drupal project.

Composer Audit reveals:
```
firebase/php-jwt
CVE-2025-45769
Affected versions: <7.0.0

guzzlehttp/guzzle
CVE-2026-55767
Affected versions: <7.12.1
Reported at: 2026-06-18T14:12:49+00:00 

guzzlehttp/guzzle
CVE-2026-55568
Affected versions: <7.12.1
Reported at: 2026-06-18T14:12:49+00:00

guzzlehttp/psr7
CVE-2026-55766
Affected versions: <2.12.1
Reported at: 2026-06-18T09:49:37+00:00
```

A `composer update -W --dry-run` does:
```
  - Upgrading firebase/php-jwt (v6.11.1 => 6.x-dev d1e91ec)
  - Upgrading guzzlehttp/guzzle (7.9.3 => 7.9.x-dev 7b2f29f)
  - Upgrading guzzlehttp/psr7 (2.10.4 => 2.10.x-dev d2a1a09)
```

Whereas `composer update -W --dry-run --no-security-blocking` does not upgrade anything.

The commit for both 7.9.3 and 7.9.x-dev are the exact same `7b2f29f`. Why is it preferring the dev version when the blocking policy is on? Is it because dev versions are not picked up or considered in the "affected version" range of a CVE, and composer assumes the dev version is not affected? Is this addressed in composer 2.10+?

title: RFC: Dependency Lifecycle Scripts
author: Tak-Pesar
body:
# Problem

Composer currently executes scripts only from the root package. Dependency packages cannot declare installation hooks that execute when the package itself is installed, updated, or removed.

This makes it difficult for packages that need to:

* Compile native extensions
* Install platform-specific binaries
* Download external assets
* Generate bindings
* Perform one-time setup tasks

The current solution is to implement a Composer plugin, which is significantly more complex than simple lifecycle scripts.

## Proposal

Introduce a new dependency-scoped lifecycle event system.

Example:

```json
{
    "scripts": {
        "on-package-install": [
            "bash ext/install.sh"
        ],
        "on-package-update": [
            "bash ext/install.sh"
        ],
        "on-package-remove": [
            "bash ext/uninstall.sh"
        ]
    }
}
```

These scripts would execute only for the package that declares them.

## Alternative Naming

Possible event names:

```json
{
    "scripts": {
        "package-install-cmd": [],
        "package-update-cmd": [],
        "package-remove-cmd": []
    }
}
```

or

```json
{
    "scripts": {
        "dependency-install-cmd": [],
        "dependency-update-cmd": [],
        "dependency-remove-cmd": []
    }
}
```

## Benefits

* Simpler than creating a Composer plugin
* Easier onboarding for package maintainers
* Explicit security approval
* Better support for native extensions and binary distributions
* Preserves Composer's current security guarantees

## Example Use Cases

* Rust extensions
* PHP native extensions
* WASM runtimes
* Binary downloads
* Code generation
* Platform-specific package setup

title: `policy.advisories.block` blocks non-affected versions when symfony/flex truncates the pool (SYMFONY_REQUIRE)
author: GromNaN
body:
When `symfony/flex`'s `SYMFONY_REQUIRE` is set, Composer 2.10 advisory blocking (`policy.advisories.block`) blocks **all** versions matching the root constraint (including versions *outside* the advisory's affected range) and fails resolution. Without Flex, the exact same setup correctly resolves to a non-affected version.

## Environment
- Composer 2.10.1
- PHP 8.5.2
- symfony/flex 2.11.0 (also reproduced with 2.10.0)

## Reproducer
`composer.json`:
```json
{
    "require-dev": {
        "symfony/flex": "^2.11",
        "symfony/cache": "^7.0"
    },
    "config": { "allow-plugins": { "symfony/flex": true } }
}
```
```bash
# A) OK -> resolves to symfony/cache v7.4.13 (safe)
composer update

# B) FAILS -> every version reported as blocked
SYMFONY_REQUIRE=7.4 composer update
```
Output of B:
```
- Root composer.json requires symfony/cache ^7.0, found symfony/cache[v7.0.0, ..., v7.4.13]
  but these were not loaded, because they are affected by security advisories ("PKSA-z7t6-zt6p-wtng").
```
Advisory `PKSA-z7t6-zt6p-wtng` (CVE-2026-45073) affects `>=7.4.0,<7.4.12`. `v7.4.12`/`v7.4.13` are **not** affected, yet they appear in the "not loaded" set.

## Expected
With `SYMFONY_REQUIRE=7.4`, resolve to `symfony/cache v7.4.13` (highest safe version in `7.4.*`), as it does without Flex.

## Diagnosis
Advisory blocking and Flex both act on pool creation; Flex registers `Symfony\Flex\Flex->truncatePackages` on `pre-pool-create` to restrict symfony/* to `SYMFONY_REQUIRE`. When Flex truncates the pool, the **per-version** advisory filtering is lost: instead of removing only `<7.4.12`, the whole constrained range is treated as affected.

Secondary symptom (same `^7.0` constraint): if the advisory is ignored via `policy.advisories.ignore-id`, with Flex active Composer resolves to the **lowest** match `v7.4.0` instead of the highest `v7.4.13` — pool truncation also disturbs version ordering under the advisory code path.

## Project Impact
Breaks doctrine/mongodb-odm CI: https://github.com/doctrine/mongodb-odm/actions/runs/27574793294/job/81520218663?pr=3007

title: composer/installers: Use it in composer.json of parent or in the plugins as well?
author: Crizz0

title: getcomposer.org installer downloads: hosting availability in CI
author: Jimbolino
body:
## Summary

We see intermittent CI failures when downloading Composer via the documented URLs (`https://getcomposer.org/installer`, phar files under `https://getcomposer.org/download/`). This started recently; failures are network timeouts to `getcomposer.org`, not checksum or installer logic errors.

DNS currently shows a single IPv4 and single IPv6 address (OVH, Dunkirk, FR). Response headers show plain nginx with no CDN cache headers.

## Context

Official install paths:

- https://getcomposer.org/download/
- https://getcomposer.org/doc/faqs/how-to-install-composer-programmatically.md

The programmatic FAQ documents alternatives, but many tools still default to `getcomposer.org` URLs.

## What we observe

On GitLab public CI runners, builds occasionally fail with:

```
curl: (28) Connection timed out after 300324 milliseconds
```

when fetching `https://getcomposer.org/installer`.

For comparison, `composer.github.io/installer.sig` serves from multiple A records with Varnish cache headers (`via: 1.1
varnish`, `x-cache: HIT`).

## Existing issues checked

No open issue on `composer/composer` about CDN or hosting availability for `getcomposer.org`. Closest related:

- [composer/composer#12858](https://github.com/composer/composer/issues/12858) (closed) - connection error handling
  during `self-update`

## Question

Were there recent server or hosting changes for `getcomposer.org`? We only started seeing these timeouts recently.

We can work around it (pinned GitHub raw URLs). However we prefer to use the official install paths so we always get the latest stable release.

title: Documentation: Write a best practices guide for caching on various platforms
author: naderman
body: There should be an article on getcomposer.org docs with best practices on configuring composer caching on CI for GitHub, bitbucket, GitLab, circleci, etc and for using composer caches in docker containers for example. And general advice on what to keep in mind, e.g. cache vendor dir vs composer cache dir.
